lever.lever: 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension (46 states + DC by Mar 2024)
lever.movement_note: The bars pair 2022 against 2023. White, Hispanic, and Asian rates all fell, but the Black rate held near 50 per 100k — roughly 3.5x the White rate — so the average moved while the gap did not. The overall rate fell to 18.6 in 2023 (669 deaths vs 817 in 2022); that improvement was statistically significant for White and Hispanic women but not for Black women. Note the timing: the 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension mostly postdates this 2023 window, so it's the forward lever aimed at the unmoved gap, not the cause of the average's improvement.

The bars pair 2022 (the pandemic trough that triggered tutoring mandates) against 2024. Nationally, grade-8 math didn't recover: White held flat at 284, Black slipped 252 to 251, and Hispanic fell four points (261 to 257), widening the White-Hispanic gap. Only Asian/Pacific Islander ticked up. Measurable recovery showed up mainly in grade 4 and in states that mandated tutoring — Louisiana beat its pre-pandemic grade-4 math, Alabama posted the largest gain — so the lever moved where deployed, but the national grade-8 picture is non-recovery, not proof it worked.

lever.lever: OTC naloxone (Narcan approved Mar 2023) + pharmacy dispensing scale-up
lever.movement_note: Deaths fell 26.2% to 79,384 in 2024 — the largest one-year drop on record, and every group fell. But naloxone was deployed concurrently with large supply-side shifts (fentanyl saturation, precursor changes) that can dwarf harm-reduction levers in top-line mortality. The data's role here is evaluative — it detected the shift — more than it is proof the lever caused it. The American Indian/Alaska Native rate is also likely understated: racial misclassification on death certificates chronically undercounts AI/AN deaths.

2026.note: Not reportable — completer count suppressed for privacy
Not reportable — completer count suppressed for privacy
Not reportable — completer count suppressed for privacy
Not reportable — completer count suppressed for privacy

lever.lever: College Scorecard publishes median earnings by specific major at specific school (field-of-study API)
lever.movement_note: Pulled straight from the College Scorecard field-of-study API — CS-family bachelor's, median earnings one year after completion. Two things surface the moment you touch the raw data. Coverage: of seven well-known CS schools, only three report a figure at all — Stanford 199,761, Harvard 158,673, Carnegie Mellon 138,781 — while Brown, MIT, CUNY York, and Fayetteville State are suppressed because too few completers make the count publishable. A tool built to help families compare programs returns a blank for exactly the regional schools they'd most want to check. Reproducibility: popular 'highest-paid CS' rankings (the ~256k Harvard figure this panel first showed) can't be reproduced from the primary data at all, and even the three reporting schools mix definitions — Harvard and CMU report under the broad 'Computer and Information Sciences, General', Stanford under 'Computer Science' proper. Whether publishing earnings changes behavior is separately unsettled: early research found a small, advantage-concentrated shift in where students sent scores; later replications found little sorting toward higher-earning schools.

lever.lever: Virginia's Longitudinal Data System links records across five state agencies (K-12, higher ed, employment, community colleges, IT) to measure post-completion wages
lever.movement_note: The aggregate is decision-grade and public — it shows some technical associate degrees out-earn liberal-arts bachelor's, upending 'higher credential = higher pay.' But the record-level data that would let families and researchers interrogate it stays governed behind data-sharing agreements. Built to decide; not built to browse.
